notes: Famous for resonant shattering with sustained pure tones. Frequency depends on glass size, thickness, and fill level. A 230ml crystal glass typically resonates near 1050 Hz. Running a wet finger around the rim excites this mode (glass harmonica effect).
Water mass-loads the glass, dropping resonant frequency significantly. Filling level precisely controls pitch — used in glass armonica instruments.

The main air resonance (Helmholtz) of an acoustic guitar body typically sits near G2 (98 Hz). The top plate 'breathing' mode is often slightly higher. These body resonances give acoustic guitars their warmth and sustain in the bass/low-mid range.
The main top plate mode. Luthiers tune this by thinning the plate. Governs midrange projection and tone quality.
Lowest note on a standard 88-key piano. String length ~2 m in a concert grand. Very long sustain due to high Q.
Highest note on a standard piano. Very short string (~50 mm), nearly inharmonic overtones due to stiffness.

mode_type: Complex shell vibration — hum, prime, tierce, quint, nominal
notes: Church bells have a complex inharmonic spectrum. The five principal partials are: hum (1×), prime (2×), minor third/tierce (2.4×), quint (3×), nominal (4×). High Q gives the characteristic long, shimmering sustain. Large European carillon bells can decay over 60 seconds.

32,768 Hz = 2¹⁵, chosen so 15 binary divisions give exactly 1 Hz for timekeeping. Exceptionally high Q means very low frequency drift. Temperature coefficient of AT-cut is near zero at 25°C. Piezoelectric effect drives and senses vibration.
Common 10 MHz oscillator for electronics. Q up to 10^6. Used in computers, communications, GPS receivers. Frequency stability to ±10 ppm or better without temperature control.

Concert pitch reference. The symmetric tine motion cancels lateral vibration, transmitting energy to the stem for coupling to surfaces. Overtones at 2.76× and 5.40× fundamental (inharmonic due to beam geometry). Frequency drifts ~-0.06 Hz/°C.
Scientific or 'philosophical' pitch where C4=256 Hz. Used in some physics labs and Verdi pitch A=432 Hz systems (256 is an exact power of 2: 2^8).

mode_type: Complex shell vibration with fluid coupling
notes: Skull resonance is relevant to bone-conduction audio devices, transcranial ultrasound, and traumatic brain injury research. The diploe (cancellous bone layer) provides significant damping. Resonance frequency shifts with age as bone density changes. Used in forensic acoustic identification.

ISO 2631 standard addresses human response to whole-body vibration. The 4-8 Hz band is most harmful for seated occupational exposure. Organ resonances: eyes ~20-90 Hz, head 20-30 Hz, chest cavity 50-100 Hz. Resonance can cause motion sickness, spinal damage, and visual impairment in vehicle operators.

The Tacoma Narrows Bridge (1940) failed at ~0.2 Hz torsional resonance driven by Kármán vortex shedding at 42 mph wind. Modern bridges use tuned mass dampers and aerodynamic sections. The Millennium Bridge, London (2000) exhibited pedestrian-induced lateral resonance at 1.04 Hz and was closed for remediation.

Natural sway frequency scales approximately as 0.1/N Hz where N=number of stories (empirical rule). A 50-story building has ~0.2 Hz fundamental. Tuned mass dampers (e.g., Taipei 101's 660-tonne pendulum) add damping to reduce occupant discomfort during wind or seismic excitation.

Automotive NVH (noise, vibration, harshness) engineering targets these frequencies. The first global bending mode is typically 25-45 Hz, torsion 25-35 Hz. Interior acoustic resonances (boom) occur 30-100 Hz. Subwoofer frequencies in car audio can excite body panels. Structural damping materials (viscoelastic sheets) are applied to panels.

The 'acoustic fatigue' from jet engine noise (1-10 kHz) has caused fuselage skin cracking historically. Interior cabin acoustic modes (20-200 Hz) are damped with insulation. The fundamental bending mode of large aircraft (747-class) is ~1-3 Hz. Flutter analysis covers 0-200 Hz. Structural health monitoring uses vibrational signatures.

A circular drum membrane has Bessel function mode shapes. The (0,1) axisymmetric mode is the fundamental. Higher modes are inharmonic with ratios 1:1.59:2.14:2.30:2.65 (not integer). Snare wire resonates sympathetically near 150-200 Hz. Tension adjustment tunes the drum.

material: Seven-metal alloy (traditional): gold, silver, copper, iron, tin, mercury, lead
notes: Tibetan singing bowls produce multiple simultaneous resonances. A 20cm bowl typically has a fundamental near 220 Hz (A3) with many overtones. The long sustain (high Q) comes from the metal alloy composition. Played by striking or by circular friction of a wooden mallet around the rim. Used in meditation, sound healing, and acoustic research.

The vocal tract is a resonant tube of ~17 cm. Formants are resonant frequencies: F1 (500-800 Hz) determines vowel openness, F2 (1000-2500 Hz) determines front/back, F3 (2500-3500 Hz) provides voice quality/timbre. Soprano singers adjust formants to amplify harmonics of the singing voice (singer's formant ~3 kHz). Male F0 = 85-180 Hz, female 165-255 Hz.
